message.content:
<teammate-message teammate_id="scott-wlaschin" color="purple" summary="Registration form accessibility heads up for Task 4">
Heydon, Scott here -- the domain architect. We're starting Task #4 (User Registration) and I wanted to loop you in early.

From US-1, the registration form needs:
- Fields: email, password, password confirmation
- Every input has a visible `<label>` element
- Validation errors shown inline next to the relevant field
- Validation errors associated with inputs via `aria-describedby`
- Form works without JavaScript (standard HTML form submission)

The domain validation I'm guiding will produce specific error types:
- `AuthError::InvalidEmail(reason)` -- email format errors
- Password too short (< 8 chars)
- Passwords don't match

These error types will map to the inline error messages. Each error carries enough information to produce a meaningful, accessible error message associated with the right field.

When the team gets to the template layer, your guidance on the `aria-describedby` pattern and the `aria-live` region for validation feedback will be essential. Just giving you a heads up so you can start thinking about the HTML structure.
</teammate-message>

input.content:
Scott, good timing -- I've already done this work. I sent Kent the complete HTML specification and testable assertions for the registration form earlier today. Let me give you the condensed version that's relevant to your domain error types. **The error-to-HTML mapping you need:** Your domain errors map to specific form fields via a struct (or HashMap) that the template receives: ```rust // What the template needs struct FormErrors { email: Option, // linked to #email-error password: Option, // linked to #password-error password_confirm: Option, // linked to #password-confirm-error general: Option, // for non-field-specific errors (top of form) } ``` The mapping from domain errors to user-facing messages: | Domain Error | Field | User-Facing Message | |---|---|---| | `AuthError::InvalidEmail(Empty)` | email | "Please enter your email" | | `AuthError::InvalidEmail(MissingAtSymbol)` | email | "Please enter a valid email address" | | `AuthError::InvalidEmail(TooLong)` | email | "Email is too long" | | `AuthError::PasswordTooShort` | password | "Password must be at least 8 characters" | | `AuthError::PasswordTooLong` | password | "Password must be less than 128 characters" | | `AuthError::PasswordMismatch` | password_confirm | "Passwords don't match" | | `AuthError::DuplicateEmail` | general | "Unable to create account. If you already have an account, try signing in." | | `AuthError::Unexpected` | general | "Something went wrong. Please try again." | **Critical point on DuplicateEmail**: The user-facing message must NOT reveal that the email exists. "An account with this email already exists" is an account enumeration vulnerability. The generic message with a link to sign in is secure AND helpful. **The HTML pattern for each field:** ```html

{{ errors.email if present }}

``` The `aria-describedby="email-error"` links the input to the error element. When the error text appears, `role="alert"` triggers an immediate screen reader announcement. The error element is always in the DOM (even when empty) so the reference is always valid. **One type-driven suggestion for you**: Consider making the `FormErrors` struct carry `Option` where `FieldError` knows both the message AND the field ID it belongs to. This makes the template rendering mechanical -- iterate errors, each one knows where it goes. It also makes it impossible to put an email error next to the password field, which is the kind of "make illegal states unrepresentable" thinking you'd appreciate. I'm ready to review the template as soon as it's created. The full HTML spec is with Kent.
input.summary: Error-to-HTML mapping and form accessibility pattern

content.text:
Good -- Scott is now aligned with the same error-to-HTML mapping that Kent and Luca already have. The domain architect, the TDD coach, and the lead engineer all have a consistent specification for how form errors flow from domain types through to accessible HTML. That's the whole pipeline: `AuthError` variant -> `FormErrors` struct -> Askama template -> `aria-describedby` + `role="alert"` -> screen reader announcement.

The team is converging. My work is seeded into every layer of the stack.
